Evaluation

This app prepares you for AT2 Task 4. First use the Learn tab to master the tools and frameworks. Then switch to the AI Tutor to build your individual Business Model Evaluation with Socratic coaching — questions that help you think, not answers that do the work for you.

How to use this resource
📚
Learn the tools
Work through the six Learn topics to understand the BMC, analysis frameworks, and what each criterion is asking for.
🤖
Use the AI Tutor
Switch to the AI Tutor tab. Choose a section of your evaluation and work through it with guided questioning.
✍️
Write your evaluation
Use your answers from the tutor conversations to draft each section. 2000 words max, or 12 minutes if oral.
Learn topics — click to begin
Topic 01 · AT2 Task 4

Understanding the Task

Before you can write a strong evaluation, you need to understand exactly what is being assessed — and what the difference between a C and an A looks like.

CA1 · CA2 · CA3 · AE1 · AE2 · AE3
What the task is asking you to do

AT2 Task 4 has two components. First, you (collaboratively or independently) develop a Business Model — built on the Business Model Canvas and supported by tools including the Lean Validation Board, Value Proposition Canvas, Customer Archetypes, Market Analysis, and Get/Keep/Grow Cycle.

Second, and most importantly for this app, you write an individual Business Model Evaluation. This is the assessed piece. It must be your own voice and your own analysis — not a group document. Maximum 2000 words (written), 12 minutes (oral), or equivalent multimodal.

The evaluation must cover three things: (1) how effective your decision-making and project management tools were, (2) the risks and opportunities facing your business including digital technology risks, and (3) your individual contribution to developing the model.

What each criterion is actually asking
🧭 CA1 — Decision-Making & Project Management Tools CA1 ▼

What it's asking: Did you use the tools (PESTLE, SWOT, BMC, Lean Board etc.) effectively and in the right context? Can you explain why you chose each tool and reflect on how useful it actually was?

A-grade language: "Perceptive and highly effective contextual application." That means: you explain not just what the tool is, but why you used it at that point, what decision it informed, and whether it was the right choice.

📊 CA2 — Creating & Applying Business Intelligence CA2 ▼

What it's asking: Did you gather real data (surveys, interviews, market research) and use it to make decisions that changed or improved your model? This is about the iterative process — pivoting based on evidence.

A-grade language: "Astute creation and application." That means: you show specific examples of data you collected, what it told you, and how it caused you to change something in the model.

🗣️ CA3 — Communication & Collaboration CA3 ▼

What it's asking: Is your evaluation clearly communicated? Is your language professional and appropriate? If you worked in a group, how did you collaborate and what was your specific role?

A-grade language: "Perceptive contextual application." That means: your writing is precise, uses correct business terminology, and your structure helps the reader follow your argument.

⚖️ AE1 — Evaluate Business Models & Plans AE1 ▼

What it's asking: Can you make a genuine judgment about whether your business model is strong or weak — and back it up with evidence? This is not a description. It's an evaluation with a clear position.

A-grade language: "Discerning evaluation." That means: you identify specific strengths and weaknesses, weigh them against each other, and arrive at a justified recommendation.

💻 AE2 — Digital Age Opportunities & Challenges AE2 ▼

What it's asking: What specific opportunities and risks does digital technology create for your business? This could be in your marketing, operations, competitive landscape, or customer experience.

A-grade language: "Critical analysis and evaluation." That means: you don't just list opportunities — you evaluate their significance, weigh them against the challenges, and draw a conclusion.

🌍 AE3 — Social, Economic, Environmental & Ethical Impacts AE3 ▼

What it's asking: What broader impacts does your business have — on the community, economy, environment, and ethical standards? Are these positive or negative? How will you manage them?

A-grade language: "Insightful analysis and evaluation." That means: you connect your specific business decisions to real-world impacts and show awareness of responsibilities beyond just making profit.

Check your understanding
QUIZEvaluation vs Description
A student writes: "We used a SWOT analysis to identify our strengths and weaknesses." Which performance level does this most closely represent?
Topic 02 · Business Model Canvas

The Business Model Canvas

The BMC is the core framework for your business model. At Stage 2, you need to go beyond filling in boxes — you need to show how the elements connect and create value together.

CA2 · AE1
The 7 elements — click each to expand
👥 Customer Segments & Value Proposition ▼

Customer Segments: Who are your customers? Be specific — not "young people" but a defined group with shared characteristics, behaviours, and needs. At Stage 2, you should have Customer Archetypes to back this up.

Value Proposition: What problem does your business solve? What value does it deliver that customers can't easily get elsewhere? This is the heart of your model — every other element should connect back to it.

  • What does the customer gain?
  • What pain does it relieve?
  • Why would they choose you over alternatives?
🤝 Customer & Stakeholder Engagement and Retention ▼

How does your business build and maintain relationships with customers and stakeholders (suppliers, partners, investors, community)? This includes both physical and digital strategies.

  • How do you acquire new customers?
  • How do you keep them coming back? (Get/Keep/Grow connects here)
  • How do you engage key stakeholders?
  • What role does digital technology play in these relationships?
🚚 Distribution Channels ▼

How does your product or service reach the customer? Channels can be physical (retail, direct sales) or digital (website, app, social commerce). At Stage 2, consider how digital channels create new opportunities or risks.

  • Direct vs indirect distribution
  • Online vs physical presence
  • Cost implications of each channel
  • Which channels reach your specific customer segment?
💰 Revenue Streams ▼

How does the business make money from delivering its value proposition? Revenue models can include: product sales, subscription fees, service fees, advertising, licensing, freemium, commission, or data monetisation.

  • Is the pricing realistic for your customer segment?
  • Are there multiple revenue streams or just one?
  • How does revenue cover costs? (connects to break-even)
🌟 Key Stakeholders ▼

Who are the most important partners outside the business — suppliers, distributors, technology providers, government bodies, investors? At Stage 2, consider how your stakeholder relationships affect risk and opportunity.

  • What do these partners provide that you can't do yourself?
  • What happens if a key stakeholder relationship breaks down?
  • Are there ethical or community stakeholders to consider? (AE3)
⚙️ Key Resources & Key Activities ▼

Key Resources: What assets does the business need to operate — physical, intellectual, human, financial? Consider whether digital assets (data, platforms, IP) are resources for your business.

Key Activities: What are the core processes that create and deliver your value proposition? For a product business this might be manufacturing; for a digital business, platform development or content creation.

The BMC at a glance
Strategyzer Business Model Canvas — the standard framework for AT2 Task 4
💡 Stage 2 thinking: Don't just describe each element in isolation. A strong evaluation shows how the elements connect — e.g. "Our customer archetype research revealed that our primary segment prefers digital channels, which directly shaped our distribution strategy and revenue model."
Check your understanding
QUIZBMC connections
A student's business sells handmade candles directly on Instagram. Their customer segment is "women aged 25–40 who value sustainability." Which BMC element most directly justifies their choice to use Instagram as the only channel?
Topic 03 · Analysis Tools

Analysis Tools

These tools are how you generate evidence to support your evaluation. The key at Stage 2 is not just knowing what each tool is — it's explaining what decision it informed and whether it was the right tool for that decision.

CA1 · AE1 · AE2 · AE3
PESTLE Analysis

PESTLE examines the external environment through six lenses: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal, Environmental. It's used to identify macro-level factors that could affect your business model — opportunities you can exploit or risks you need to manage.

At Stage 2, PESTLE connects strongly to AE2 (Technological factors — digital age opportunities and challenges) and AE3 (Social, Economic, Environmental factors — broader impacts on society and environment).

⚠️ Common mistake: Students list PESTLE factors without connecting them to their specific business. An A-grade response picks the 2–3 most significant factors and evaluates their actual impact — not just "technology is changing fast."
SWOT Analysis

SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) examines both internal factors (S/W) and external factors (O/T). In your evaluation it's most powerful when you show how internal strengths can exploit external opportunities, or how weaknesses create vulnerability to threats.

For CA1, the key question is: was SWOT the right tool to use at that point in the development process? Was it used at the beginning (to inform the model) or at the end (to evaluate it)? What specific decision did it change?

Get / Keep / Grow Cycle

The Get/Keep/Grow framework examines three stages of the customer relationship:

Get — How do you acquire new customers? What marketing channels and messages will attract your target segment?
Keep — How do you retain existing customers? What creates loyalty and reduces churn?
Grow — How do you increase the value of existing customers? Upselling, referrals, community?

This connects to the BMC's Customer Engagement and Retention element, and to AE2 where digital tools (CRM systems, social media, email automation) create new opportunities in each stage.

Check your understanding
QUIZChoosing the right tool
A student wants to evaluate whether Instagram advertising could help grow their customer base. Which tool is most directly relevant to this decision?
Reflect — applying the tools to your model
Which two analysis tools did your group use most? What specific decision did each one inform?
0 words
Example response
We primarily used PESTLE and Get/Keep/Grow. PESTLE helped us identify the Technological factor most relevant to our business — the rapid adoption of contactless payments — which led us to include a digital payment integration as a key resource. The Get/Keep/Grow framework was used after we completed our customer archetypes. It revealed that our target segment (busy parents aged 28–40) were most likely to be acquired through local Facebook community groups rather than Instagram, which caused us to shift our acquisition budget and change our distribution channel assumptions in the BMC.
PESTLE visual reference
SWOT visual reference
Get / Keep / Grow visual reference
Competitive Analysis

The Competitive Analysis Framework helps you understand how your business compares to existing competitors across four dimensions: Company Specific (size, funding, investors), Target Customer / Message (who they sell to and how), Product Specific (features, pricing, strengths/weaknesses), and Positioning (how they win and why customers choose them).

In your evaluation, competitive analysis connects to AE1 (are there risks from stronger competitors?) and AE2 (are competitors using digital technologies that could disrupt your model?). A strong response doesn't just list competitors — it evaluates what their strengths mean for your business strategy.

Competitive Analysis Framework — compare up to 3 competitors across Company, Customer, Product, and Positioning dimensions
💡 Evaluation tip: The "Positioning" section — How to Win and Why Customers Should Choose Us — is the most important part for your evaluation. If you can't clearly answer both questions, your value proposition needs more work.
Revenue Streams & Cost Structure

Two BMC elements that students often under-develop in their evaluations are Revenue Streams and Cost Structure. Together they determine whether the business model is financially viable.

Revenue Streams — how the business earns money. Common models include: direct product/service sales, subscription fees, freemium (free tier + paid upgrade), advertising, commission/marketplace, licensing, or data monetisation. At Stage 2 you should evaluate whether your revenue model is realistic for your customer segment — are they willing and able to pay that amount?

Cost Structure — the major costs involved in operating the model. These fall into fixed costs (rent, salaries, subscriptions — same regardless of volume) and variable costs (materials, shipping, transaction fees — scale with volume). A strong evaluation identifies your two or three largest costs and assesses whether the revenue model can cover them.

💰 Revenue Streams — common models for start-ups ▼
  • Direct sales — customer pays once for a product or service. Simple, but no recurring revenue.
  • Subscription — customer pays regularly (weekly/monthly/annual). Predictable recurring revenue; harder to acquire customers initially.
  • Freemium — basic version free, premium features paid. Good for building a user base; conversion rate from free to paid is the key risk.
  • Commission/marketplace — business takes a % of each transaction between buyers and sellers. Scales well; requires volume.
  • Advertising — revenue from showing ads to a free user base. Requires large audience; privacy concerns (AE3).
  • Licensing — charge others to use your IP, technology, or brand. High margin; requires protectable IP.

In your evaluation: Name your revenue model, explain why it suits your customer segment, and assess the risk of relying on it.

📉 Cost Structure — evaluating financial viability ▼
  • Fixed costs — platform hosting, rent, insurance, salaries. These exist whether you sell 0 or 1000 units. Identify yours and estimate the monthly total.
  • Variable costs — cost of goods sold, packaging, shipping, payment processing fees. These scale with sales volume.
  • Break-even point — the sales volume at which total revenue covers total costs. A strong evaluation states this and assesses whether it is achievable given your market size.
  • Burn rate — for start-ups, how quickly you spend money before becoming profitable. Relevant to your risk analysis.

In your evaluation: Identify your two largest costs and assess whether your revenue model is sufficient to cover them at a realistic sales volume.

Topic 04 · Business Intelligence

Business Intelligence

Business intelligence is the process of collecting real data, analysing it, and using it to make better decisions. CA2 is entirely about this — showing that your model was shaped by evidence, not just ideas.

CA2 · AE1
The supporting documents — click each to expand
✅ Lean Validation Board ▼

The Lean Validation Board tracks your assumptions and tests whether they are validated or invalidated by real evidence. It records: the hypothesis, the experiment or test, the results, and whether you pivoted or persevered.

In your evaluation: Identify at least one assumption that was invalidated and explain how it caused you to change the business model. This is the core of CA2 — the iterative process.

💎 Value Proposition Canvas ▼

The VPC maps the fit between your customer profile (jobs, pains, gains) and your value map (products/services, pain relievers, gain creators). It ensures your solution is solving a real problem for a real customer.

In your evaluation: Show how the VPC changed or confirmed your value proposition. If the fit wasn't strong initially, how did you iterate to improve it?

🧑 Customer Archetypes ▼

Customer archetypes (or personas) are detailed profiles of your target customers — their goals, frustrations, behaviours, and context. They are built from qualitative research (interviews, observations) not assumptions.

In your evaluation: Reference specific insights from your archetype research that influenced your model. E.g. "Our archetype research revealed that customers were more price-sensitive than we assumed, leading us to introduce a freemium tier."

📈 Market Analysis ▼

Market analysis quantifies the size and structure of your market — TAM (Total Addressable Market), SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market), SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market). It also covers competitor analysis and market trends.

In your evaluation: Use market data to justify key assumptions in your model — pricing, target segment size, channel selection. Connect market trends to your AE2 and AE3 analysis.

💡 The pivot story: The most powerful CA2 responses tell a "pivot story" — we assumed X, we tested it with Y method, the data showed Z, so we changed W in our model. If you can't identify at least one pivot, go back to your portfolio and look for where your thinking changed.
Check your understanding
QUIZCA2 in practice
A student writes: "We conducted customer interviews and found that our target customers were more concerned about convenience than price. We updated our Value Proposition Canvas to emphasise time-saving features rather than cost." Which criterion does this best demonstrate?
Topic 05 · Writing the Evaluation

Writing the Evaluation

Structure, language, and what A-grade evaluation looks like in practice. The AI Tutor in the next tab will coach you through each section — this topic helps you understand what you're aiming for before you start.

CA3 · AE1 · AE2
Recommended structure (2000 words)
1️⃣Introduction (~150 words)▼
Briefly introduce your business model — name, concept, value proposition, and target customer. State the purpose of the evaluation. Do not waste space describing the BMC framework — get straight into your specific model.
2️⃣Decision-Making & Project Management (~400 words) → CA1▼
Evaluate the effectiveness of the tools you used. Choose 2–3 tools and for each one: explain why you used it at that point, what it revealed, what decision it informed, and how effective it actually was. Include honest reflection — if a tool had limitations, say so.
3️⃣Business Intelligence & Iteration (~400 words) → CA2▼
Describe the business intelligence you created — what data you collected, from whom, and by what method. Show at least one pivot: an assumption you held, evidence that challenged it, and how the model changed as a result. Reference your Lean Validation Board, VPC, and/or Archetypes directly.
4️⃣Risks, Opportunities & Digital Technologies (~400 words) → AE1 + AE2▼
Evaluate the overall model's risks and opportunities. Then specifically address digital technology: what opportunities does it create (new channels, automation, data), and what risks (cybersecurity, disruption, dependency)? Make a recommendation to improve the model based on this analysis.
5️⃣Social, Economic, Environmental & Ethical Impact (~300 words) → AE3▼
Analyse the broader impacts of your business. Be specific — not "all businesses affect the environment" but what your business does, who it affects, and what you're doing or planning to do about it. Include at least two of the four impact areas.
6️⃣Individual Contribution (~200 words) → CA3▼
If you worked collaboratively: describe your specific role in the model's development, what tasks you led, what decisions you made, and how you collaborated. Be honest — vague claims about "contributing to everything" read as weak. Specificity earns marks.
7️⃣Conclusion (~150 words)▼
Summarise your overall evaluation of the business model's viability. This is not just a repeat of what you've said — it's a final judgment backed by your strongest evidence. End with a forward-looking recommendation.
🎯 The A-grade difference: Descriptions say what happened. Evaluations say whether it was effective and why. Every paragraph should end with a judgment, not a description. Ask yourself: "So what? What does this mean for the business?"
Check your understanding
QUIZEvaluation language
Which sentence best demonstrates AE1 (evaluate business models)?
Topic 06 · AE2 & AE3

Digital Age & Broader Impacts

AE2 and AE3 are where many students lose marks by staying too general. This topic helps you apply these criteria precisely to your specific business.

AE2 · AE3
AE2 — Opportunities & challenges in the digital age

AE2 is not just "technology is important for modern business." It requires you to identify specific digital technologies relevant to your business, evaluate whether they are an opportunity or a challenge (or both), and draw a conclusion about what your business should do.

Think across four areas: Operations (automation, AI, cloud tools), Marketing (social media, SEO, email, influencers), Customer experience (apps, chatbots, personalisation), and Risk (cybersecurity, data privacy, platform dependency, digital disruption from competitors).

⚠️ A-grade AE2 structure: Name the technology → explain the specific opportunity or challenge it creates for your business → weigh its significance → recommend a course of action. Don't just list technologies — evaluate them.
AE3 — Social, economic, environmental & ethical impacts

AE3 asks you to think beyond profit. Every business has impacts — positive and negative — on the community, economy, environment, and ethical standards. An A-grade response identifies impacts that are specifically relevant to your business (not generic), evaluates their significance, and shows awareness of responsibilities.

Social: Employment, community benefit, accessibility, health, wellbeing.
Economic: Local vs global supply chains, pricing equity, competition effects, tax obligations.
Environmental: Carbon footprint, waste, packaging, energy use, supply chain sustainability.
Ethical: Data privacy, labour practices, fair trade, transparency, advertising honesty.

Check your understanding
QUIZAE2 vs AE3
A student's business collects personal health data from users via an app. "The app creates a privacy risk because it stores sensitive health data — we need to comply with Australia's Privacy Act and implement end-to-end encryption." Which criterion does this address?
Reflect — your AE2 and AE3 content
Identify one specific digital opportunity and one social/environmental impact relevant to your business. Evaluate the significance of each in 3–4 sentences.
0 words
Example response
Digital opportunity: Social media advertising via Instagram and TikTok represents a significant opportunity for our business because our customer archetype (women aged 22–35 interested in sustainable fashion) are heavy users of both platforms. AI-powered audience targeting could allow us to reach this segment with precision at a low cost-per-acquisition compared to traditional advertising — a critical advantage for a bootstrapped start-up. However, we are dependent on platform algorithms that could change without warning, which creates channel risk.

Environmental impact: Our product requires physical packaging, which generates waste. As our customer segment values sustainability, this creates both an ethical obligation and a brand risk. We have committed to using compostable packaging, which increases our cost per unit by $0.40 but aligns with our value proposition and reduces the risk of customer backlash on social media.

🎉 Learn section complete

You've worked through all six topics. You now understand the BMC, the analysis tools, how business intelligence drives CA2, the evaluation structure, and what A-grade AE2 and AE3 looks like.
